coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots from Ananas comosus waste peels: A promising material for NLO behaviour, antibacterial, and antioxidant activities
coredata.dc:creator: Surendran, P.
Lakshmanan, A.
Priya, S. Sakthy
Geetha, P.
Rameshkumar, P.
Kannan, Karthik
Hegde, Tejaswi Ashok
Vinitha, G.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized from agro-waste Ananas comosus by facile hydrothermal treatment. The synthesize CQDs are characterized by XRD, HRTEM, FTIR, XPS, UV–Visible spectral analysis, and Quantum yield (QY) measurements. The formation of CQDs has a typical particle size of 2.4 ± 0.5 nm which was measured by HR-TEM analysis. The fluorescence properties of CQDs show strong blue emission radiation with a QY of 10.65%. The NLO CQDs exhibit properties of self-defocusing and multi-photon absorbance when exposed to a continuous-wave laser (532 nm, 100 mW). The calculated third-order NLO parameters, like β, n2, and χ(3) was obtained to be in the order of 3.0864 × 10-4 cm/W, 8.5137 × 10-9 cm2/W, and 3.2882 × 10-7 (esu), respectively. Antibacterial activities of CQDs were screened against (S. aureus, B. cereus, E. coli, P. aeruginosa, and V. cholera) microbial organisms and Antioxidant assay of the CQDs was assessed against the DPPH method. Furthermore, the synthesized CQDs will be applicable to NLO devices and pharmaceutical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Facile hydrothermal treatment
CQDs
Fluorescence quantum yield
Z-scan analysis
Antibacterial activity
Antioxidant assay

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: PXRD pattern of the synthesized CQDs.
HR-TEM image of as-synthesized CQDs. (a, b) HR-TEM image, (c) HR-TEM of one representative CQDs, (d) Histogram image of CQDs with an particle size distributions.
Survey XPS spectra of CQDs (a) high resolution XPS data of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) O1s.
Linear refractive index and extinction co-efficient of synthesized CQDs.
Fluorescence spectra of CQDs at two excitation wavelengths.
Fluorescence spectra of CQDs at different concentrations.
(a) Closed aperture (b) Open aperture Z-scan pattern of CQDs.
Antibacterial plate photos of Ananas comosus CQDs against human bacterial pathogens.
Possible mechanisms responsible for the antibacterial activity of Ananas comosus CQDs.

(a) DPPH assay (2, 2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) (b) Super oxide anion radical scavenging activity (c) Hydrogen peroxide radical scavenging activity (d) Hydrogen peroxide radical scavenging activity of carbon quantum dots at different concentrations.
Schematic diagram of the synthesized carbon quantum dots.
Third order NLO parameter values of synthesis CQDs.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots from Ananas comosus waste peels: A promising material for NLO behaviour, antibacterial, and antioxidant activities

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: PG and Research Department of Physics, Periyar E.V.R. College (Autonomous), Affiliated to Bharathidasan University, Tiruchirappalli 620 023, Tamilnadu, India

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: P. Surendran and A. Lakshmanan have equally contributed as first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized from agro-waste Ananas comosus by facile hydrothermal treatment. The synthesize CQDs are characterized by XRD, HRTEM, FTIR, XPS, UV–Visible spectral analysis, and Quantum yield (QY) measurements. The formation of CQDs has a typical particle size of 2.4 ± 0.5 nm which was measured by HR-TEM analysis. The fluorescence properties of CQDs show strong blue emission radiation with a QY of 10.65%. The NLO CQDs exhibit properties of self-defocusing and multi-photon absorbance when exposed to a continuous-wave laser (532 nm, 100 mW). The calculated third-order NLO parameters, like β, n2, and χ(3) was obtained to be in the order of 3.0864 × 10-4 cm/W, 8.5137 × 10-9 cm2/W, and 3.2882 × 10-7 (esu), respectively. Antibacterial activities of CQDs were screened against (S. aureus, B. cereus, E. coli, P. aeruginosa, and V. cholera) microbial organisms and Antioxidant assay of the CQDs was assessed against the DPPH method. Furthermore, the synthesized CQDs will be applicable to NLO devices and pharmaceutical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: In particular, the utilization of waste in the vegetable and fruit production sectors presents a vital challenge to governments as well as farmers. Hence, there is a need to address these issues towards sustainability [1]. A large amount of fruit and vegetable waste can be processed in a useful manner by transforming it into value-added products. In reality, farming with unwanted wastes is no longer regarded as a viable substitute [2]. Furthermore, growing demands for this merchandise as feed and based on farming yields that are efficient, less expensive, and ecologically pleasant usages of these substances have emerged as greater necessary [3]. According to Oreopoulou and Russ et al. [4] due to new advances in process engineering and the subsequent by-product, there is a growing curiosity in the recovery of waste generated by the food and agricultural production sectors. Pineapple processing generates solid residue in amounts as high as 75–80% [1,5]. Pineapple factory waste has been observed to have practicable use is a fresh material to acquire esteemed products, because it has uncomplicated and complex sugars which might also be used additionally in the manufacturing of metabolites like citric acid, vinegar, and ethanol [1,6–9]. It is also a source of other bio-active compounds for example antioxidants [10]. Pineapple dregs might also incorporate the proteolytic enzymes, usually isolated from the stem (EC 3.4.22.33), as well as found in the ripeness fruit [11]. The most significant agricultural commodity in India is pineapples (Ananas comosus). Pineapple peel waste has become an important issue in waste management, and it is desirable to convert it into a value-added product. It has potentially useful ingredients such as sugars, a phenolic compound, high fibre, and protein, as well as anti-oxidant properties. It also provides a high potential bromelain enzyme as a functional material [12]. Pineapple peel waste can also be synthesized to form CQDs, and this can be accomplished by inexpensive and eco-friendly techniques. The CDs synthesis methods are separated into two parts: (i) top-down and (ii) bottom-up method [13,14]. The bottom-up method has a benefit in changing the structure and physical properties of CQDs by choosing the correct organic precursors, and carbonization conditions. One of the simplest, direct, and efficient ways to synthesize CQDs under the bottom-up method (hydrothermal method) [15].

The impact of quantum confinement is high surface flexibility and as easy solution-based processability that can be used for optical tools. CQDs have non-linear optical (NLO) properties by the strength of the confinement regime [16–18]. Carbon dots, a carbonaceous family type, have unique physical properties that have drawn considerable interest in specific fields of research. These can be also be used as an alternative to traditional semiconductor QDs and can be used with the benefits of low toxicity, chemical stiffness, flexibility, improved biocompatibility, and valued luminescence properties in future optoelectronic devices [19–21]. CQDs are typically quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with sp2 carbon, oxygen functional groups, and doping components [22–25]. In recent research, the focus was on developing eco-friendly “green” methods to synthesize CQDs using natural precursors [26–30]. CQDs derived from natural precursors are used in mass production at low cost and are biocompatible. Several researchers have noted the synthesis of quantum dots with natural precursors [31–34].

In the present work includes effortlessly available natural precursors pineapple peel waste is used to synthesize CQDs via facile hydrothermal route. The synthesized CQDs are characterized using XRD, HRTEM, FTIR, XPS, UV–Visible, and fluorescence analysis. To the exception of the authors’ knowledge, however, there are no previous reports available using Z-scan analyses for this purpose. To investigate the third-order NLO properties regarding CQDs that can be applied to optical switching and fluorescence imaging applications using Z-scan measurements and also to measure their antioxidant (DPPH assay) and antibacterial properties (clinical pathogens).

Pineapples (Ananas comosus) were collected from cultivation areas in the Salem district, Periyamangalam, at Pachamalai, in Tamil Nadu. The fresh peels of the pineapple were cut into lesser sizes and cleansed with natural river water. They were held in an open atmosphere for one week in sunlight and then combustion was done at 65 °C. After the combustion process, the pineapple waste peels converted into carbon. Merck AR grade (Ammonia and citric acid (CA)) were purchased.

The synthesis way schematic diagram is illustrated in Scheme 1 . First, 2 g carbon and 2 g CA were mixed in 25 mL of dist·H2O with continuous stirring for complete dispersion. Next, the ammonia solution was added drop-by-drop to obtain pH 7. The precursor was shifted into an autoclave at 200 °C for 6 h. Later, the autoclave was automatically cooled until it reached 30 °C. The solution was ultra-sonicated for 60 min and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 1 h at 30 °C. The supernatant liquid was obtained after the removal of the black precipitate. The supernatant solvent was then mixed with ethanol and used for further analysis. The supporting information provides a detailed description of the characterization, antibacterial activity, and antioxidant assays of the as-synthesized CQDs.

The crystallinity of the Ananas comosus CQDs was explored with the help of PXRD, as given in Fig. 1 . The XRD pattern of the CQDs exhibits a wide diffraction peak at 2θ = 22-25°, which ensures the amorphous nature of the CQDs [35,36]. The peak corresponds to the crystal lattice (002), which is a key feature for carbon materials. These interpretations are well matched with the recently reported CQDs [37,38].

HRTEM characterization was studied to structural and particle size of the prepared CQDs, which were dispersed in water and deposited in a copper grid. As shown in Fig. 2 (a) and (b), the CQDs are monodispersed particles have a narrow size distribution. The size distribution graph is shown in Fig. 2 (d), which is calculated from the HRTEM image using ImageJ software (NIH). The particle size of the as-prepared CQDs is distributed in a diameter range of 1.0–4.0 nm and has a mean diameter of 2.5 ± 0.5 nm. Moreover, it was observed from the HRTEM image shown a well-resolved lattice fringe of the CQDs is depicted in Fig. 2 (c) which is similar with the previous report [39–42]. The HR-TEM results clearly confirm that the CQDs were successfully synthesized small spherical shape.

The XPS has been analysed to test the surface elemental composition and oxidation state of the as-prepared CQDs; the components of the surface groups and the structure as-synthesized CQDs have been thoroughly studied. In addition, as shown in Fig. 3 (a), the three important peaks located at 284.13, 399, and 531.4 eV which can be ascribed to the C1s, N1s, and O1s respectively. The distribution of C1s explicitly in Fig. 3 (b) shows deconvoluted peaks of 284.13 and 285.6 eV correspond to C = C, C-N bonds. Fig. 3 (c), enunciate the nitrogen bonds, two peaks located at 399 and 397.6 eV are related with C–N–C and C–N groups. Fig. 3 (d), shows the two peaks at 531.4 and 532.6 eV, due to the presence of C–OH/C–O–C and C = O bands, in which the graphite structure of the CQDs was set by the binding energy peaks at 284.13 eV referring to the C1s. The XPS findings indicate that these CQDs are predominantly composed of carbon (65.7%), oxygen (26.9%), nitrogen (6.2%), and sulphur (1.2%). The FTIR measurement was carried out to determine the functional groups of CQDs are shown in Fig. S1.

UV–Visible spectrum (UV–Vis) was recorded for the as-prepared CQDs in the range of 200–600 nm of the CQDs as displayed in Fig. 4 . The spectrum has two absorption peaks near 236 and 333 nm. The 236 nm peak is accredited to the presence of aromatic π-π* transition of the C = C band and the related electron transitions in the oxygen contain carbon dots. The absorption peak at 333 nm is due to the n-π* transition of the C = O band [43,44]. The sample was irradiated at a higher wavelength of 365 nm using a UV light lamp, and the supernatant solution instantly produced a blue emission colour, as displayed in the inset of Fig. 4. The linear optical absorption (LOA) coefficient (α) was estimated using,
(1)
α =
2.303 × A
d
where d is the thickness of the specimen cuvette; and A is the material absorption. The reflectance (R) value of the CQDs was calculated using the following equation:
(2)
R =
e x p
- α t
±
e x p
- α t
T - e x p
- 3 α t
T + e x p
- 2 α t
T
2
e x p
- α t
+ e x p
- 2 α t
T

The parameter R can be used to easily calculate the linear refractive index (n0) of the CQDs from the following relationship:
(3)
n 0
= -
R + 1
± 2
R
( R - 1 )

Fig. 5 shows the excitation coefficient and
n 0
of the CQDs, which was found to be 1.2265 at a wavelength of 532 nm, and it can be used to find the NLO susceptibility χ(3) if the CQDs.

The fluorescent spectra of the prepared CQDs are exhibited in Fig. 6 , where the spectra are measured for two excitation wavelengths of 230 and 330 nm. The corresponding emission wavelength of the CQDs peak is 438 nm. From the fluorescence spectra, the excitation wavelength increases with an increase in the intensity, it can be observed that there is no measurable emission peak shift [45–47]. Furthermore, in the present work, we also check fluorescence studies for different concentrations of the supernatant solution. Initially, the aqueous solution of CQDs was diluted and a UV absorption measurement was taken for absorption intensities of 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, and 0.10 at an optimal excitation wavelength of 330 nm. Afterward, the dispersed solution was taken in a 10 mm cuvette, which was an insert in the integrating sphere and excited with 330 nm monochromatic light. As represented in Fig. 7 , the symmetrical emission spectrum intensity increases with an increase in absorption intensity. The high fluorescence CQDs are suitable for fluorescence imaging, optical labeling, and optoelectronic devices [48]. The QY of the prepared pineapple waste peel CQDs were calculated using standard relations [49] and was found to be 10.65%. The produced CQDs were matched with various natural precursors with a synthetic method, size, and quantum yield are summarized in Table S1 (see in SI). The prepared CQDs exhibited a larger quantum yield (%) compared to other natural precursors CQDs.

The single-beam Z-scan procedure is widely used to determine the third harmonic generation for different types of NLO materials, such as single crystal, liquid crystal, glasses, semiconductors, nanoparticles, and biological materials, which resolve the changes in the nonlinear refractive (NLR) index and nonlinear absorption (NLA) coefficient of the sample [50]. The Z-scan analysis was analyzed to determine a definite quantity for the NLA coefficient (β), the NLR index (n2), and the third-order nonlinear susceptibility χ(3), which give them various properties that lead to a kind of acoustically-operated quantum electronic devices. The synthesized CQDs were dispersed by deionized water. The dispersed sample was taken in a 1 mm cuvette, which was fixed in a sample holder, and the sample cell was moved using a conversion system from the negative to the positive z-axis in the pathway of the laser beam direction. Therefore, the sample transmittance laser intensity passed through the far-field aperture and was measured by a photodetector. As depicted in Fig. 8 (a), the closed-aperture (CA) Z-scan curve of the synthesized CQDs gives a pre-focal transmission peak and a post-focal transmission valley, which represents the self-defocusing nonlinearity of the carbon dots, i.e., the negative sign of the nonlinear refraction. Fig. 8 (b), depicts the open-aperture (OA) Z-scan curve of the carbon dot sample, which reveals a transmission valley that may be responsible for the reverse saturable absorption (RSA) coefficient effect of the CQDs [51,52]. The Z-scan studies designate the (β) and (n2); the real and imaginary parts of the higher-order NLO susceptibility can be determined using equation [53]:
(4)
Re
χ
3
e s u
=
10
- 4
ε 0
c
2
n
0
2
n
2
π
(
cm
2
/ W )
(5)
Im
χ
3
e s u
=
10
- 2
ε 0
c
2
n
0
2
λ β
4 π
2
( c m / W )
where ε0 is the vacuum permittivity; n0 is the linear refractive index of the CQDs; and C is 3 × 108 m/s. The calculated nonlinearity values are listed in Table 1 . The synthesized CQDs are well-suited for NLO-based optical devices, for example, all-optical switches if the conditions W > 1 and T < 1 are satisfied. The comparison of the NLO susceptibility χ(3) values of CQDs and some other NLO materials [52,54–56] are tabulated in (Table 2 ). The parameters W and T were evaluated to assess the NLO performance of the produced CQDs, and were calculated as follows [57]:
(6)
W =
n 2
I
α 0
λ
(7)
T =
β λ
n 2
where n2 is 8.5137 × 10-9 cm2/W; and I is 3.47 kW/cm2. To evaluate the figure of merit, using W = 16.075, and T = 0.0192 satisfies the demands. Hence, the synthesized CQDs are suitable for all-optical switches, super-continuum generation, and NLO-based optical devices.

The antimicrobial activity was performed against selected clinical isolated pathogens. The microorganism’s strain growth on the surface of the sample has been determined and its photos are presented in Fig. 9 . The most significant result of carbon quantum dots is observed to produce a 34 mm inhibition region of gram-negative bacteria against Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Table 3 shows the inhibitory zone for certain identified pathogens.

Fig. 10 , Illustrates the antibacterial mechanism of Ananas comosus CQDS. The antibacterial activity of CQDs on pathogenic organisms exists primarily by three underlying mechanisms: (i) disruption of cell walls/membranes; (ii) Generation of ROS to damage membranes; and (iii) interaction with nucleic substance (DNA/RNA) to suppress cell death [58,59]. In functionalized CQDs, the efficiency of the antibacterial effects largely depends upon various factors such as small size, zeta potential, and charge transfer effect. Yadav et al. [60] have reported that CNQDs which could effectively create hydroxyl and superoxide radicals and CNQDs interacted with gram-positive (S. aureus) and gram-negative (E. coli) pathogens. Travlou et al. [61] have developed N-doped CQDs with specific antimicrobial efficacy against E. coli and B. subtilis. Yang et al. [62] have developed quaternized CQDs via an easy carbonyl group process amine-functionalized CQDs and a quaternary ammonium complex. They observed that the CQDs obtained demonstrated effective antioxidant activity related to the specific binding of the positive charge quaternary ammonium groups to the G + Ve microbial strains –Ve charged surface. Rajendiran et al. stated that the amino-functionalized N-doped GQDs were demonstrated to be an effective photosensitizer, in which the five available nitrogen electrons could produce a high + Ve charge density on CDs [63]. The antibacterial action might be ascribed to the hydroxyl surface group and the nitrogen group found on the CQDs evident from the FTIR and XPS studies. The CQDs with a positive charge and cell membrane with a negative charge are mutually attracted and also leads to membrane integrity loss. The above results reveal that the synthesized CQDs show a remarkable activity that confirms the prepared CQDs are precise for medical devices against microbes [64–67].

The synthesized CQDs have good antioxidant capacity was estimated by using DPPH assay, Superoxide anion, hydroxyl, and hydrogen peroxide radical scavenging activities. For all antioxidant, ascorbic acid is preferred as a standard and the absorbance of the control and the sample have calculated by using the formula.
(8)
% RSA =
Abs
o f
c o n t r o l - A b s
o f
s a m p l e
Abs
o f
c o n t r o l
× 100
where, Radical Scavenging Activity (RSA); Abs. control is the absorbance of the solution without sample; Abs sample is the absorbance of the solution + CQDs. The antioxidant reacts with DPPH to a free radical with red colour that becomes yellow when scavenged. The level of discoloration shows the capacity of the antioxidant to scavenge in terms of the ability of the hydrogen to provide. The percentage of inhibition of scavenging reaction between (DPPH) and CQDs were increased while increasing the concentration (Fig. 11 a). The Superoxide dismutase (SOD) is a metalloenzyme that catalyzes the reduction of superoxide radicals into H2O2 and O2 and consequently provides a significant defense mechanism against superoxide radical toxicity [68]. In this result, the activity is also increased as represented in Fig. 11 (b). Hydroxyl radical is one of the biological systems important reactive oxygen species. This interacts with cell membrane phospholipids of polyunsaturated fatty acids and induces cell damage [69]. From these results, the hydroxyl radical (Fig. 11 c) and hydrogen peroxide scavenging activity (Fig. 11 d) were increased when compared to the standard ascorbic acid.

In summary, Ananas comosus waste peel NLO CQDs have been successfully prepared by the facile hydrothermal technique using a natural precursor, which is non-toxic, low-cost, and has enhanced QY efficiency. The CQDs were investigated to different characterization studies to observe their suitability for numerous applications. The fluorescence spectra of the CQDs displayed blue emission radiation at 438 nm, which can be used for photonic devices. Furthermore, the synthesized CQDs exhibit good nonlinearity, which fulfils the optical switching conditions W > 1 and T < 1. Our results suggest that prepared CQDs are suitable for all-optical switching and limiting devices, bio-sensor, and imaging applications. Also, it is a good candidate for antibacterial activity and antioxidant assay against clinical pathogens.

P. Surendran: Investigation, Writing - original draft, Data curation. A. Lakshmanan: Investigation, Writing - original draft, Data curation. S. Sakthy Priya: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Software. P. Geetha: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Project administration. P. Rameshkumar: Supervision, Validation, Writing - review & editing. Karthik Kannan: Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Visualization. Tejaswi Ashok Hegde: Formal analysis, Software, Writing - review & editing. G. Vinitha: Writing - review & editing.

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
